---
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
---

# Methodology {.appendix}

## Data and reproducibility

This book is built from data pulled directly from the **Neon CRM** API. Every figure is
reproducible: data is extracted, cleaned, geocoded, and summarized by the `hh` package, then read
from saved parquet tables in `data/20_processed/`. All numbers in the prose are computed from the
data at render time, so they stay correct as the data is refreshed. Every API pull is recorded in
a manifest (endpoint, filters, timestamps, row counts, file hashes, code commit), and raw extracts
are immutable — see the project README for the full provenance story.

All published charts and tables are **aggregates** (counts and dollar totals by category,
distance band, and day of week) — no individual is identified.

## About the data

The raw data is pulled from the **Neon CRM REST API (v2)** and saved exactly as Neon returns it.
There are four entities, and every record retrieved streams straight to disk — analysis never
depends on the live API.

- **Accounts** — every constituent: donors, ticket-buyers, and class-takers, as individuals and
  households. One row per Neon account. Carries identity (account id, account type, first / last /
  full / company name), household membership (household id and name), mailing address (street, city,
  state, ZIP, country), contact info (email, phone), and the **deceased** and **do-not-contact**
  flags used to filter people out.
- **Donations** — every gift. One row per donation, tied to an account by account id. Carries
  amount, date, type (`DONATION` or `PLEDBGEPAYMENT`), status (e.g. `SUCCEEDED`), and fund /
  campaign / purpose / source. It carries no household id; that link is recovered during cleaning.
- **Events** — every scheduled event: a single performance, class, or community happening. One row
  per Neon event. Carries the event name, Neon's event category, start and end dates, capacity, and
  Neon's attendee count. There is no field linking the nights of a multi-night run; runs are
  reconstructed later.
- **Registrations** — every ticket / registration purchase, linking an account to an event. One row
  per registration, with nested ticket and attendee records (used to count true headcount). Carries
  the registrant's account id, amount, date-time, and source / origin.

```{python}
#| label: raw-data-summary
import pandas as pd
from hh.io import load_raw

_raw = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "entity": ["accounts", "donations", "events", "registrations"],
        "what it is": [
            "Constituents — donors, patrons, class-takers (individuals & households)",
            "Gifts, one per donation",
            "Scheduled events — one performance, class, or community event",
            "Ticket/registration purchases linking an account to an event",
        ],
    }
)
_raw["records"] = [len(load_raw(e)) for e in _raw["entity"]]
_raw[["entity", "records", "what it is"]]
```

::: {callout-note}
Record counts are computed from the latest saved pull at render time, so they stay correct as the
data is refreshed. **Format & storage.** Each pull is written as newline-delimited JSON (`.jsonl`,
one record per line) into an immutable, timestamped folder `data/00_raw/neon/pull-<UTC>/`, alongside
a `manifest.yaml` that records the endpoint, filters, output fields, Neon-reported counts,
timestamps, file SHA-256 hashes, and the code commit. Accounts, donations, and events are pulled in
one pass (`scripts/pull.py`); registrations are gathered by a per-event sweep
(`scripts/sweep_registrations.py`) because Neon serves them event-by-event. Downstream code always
reads these saved files (`hh.io.load_raw`), never the live API.
:::

## Data cleaning

Cleaning turns the raw API records into standardized analytic tables (the `hh.clean` package). It is
purely deterministic and re-runs from the saved raw files on every `scripts/build.py`, so the
processed tables in `data/20_processed/` can always be rebuilt.

- **Column standardization.** Neon's output-field names are mapped to short `snake_case` names. Each
  entity has an explicit field map (for example, `Account ID → account_id`,
  `Donation Amount → donation_amount`); any column not in the map is converted by `vmap`, a faithful
  port of the R project's renaming function (`hh.clean.standardize`).
- **Type parsing.** Dollar amounts are coerced to numeric and dates to datetimes, with malformed
  values turned into missing rather than crashing.
- **Flag conversion.** The deceased and do-not-contact text fields (`"Yes"`) become real booleans.
- **Household rollup.** Every analytic record gets a canonical household id, name, and group
  (`hh.clean.households.add_rollup`): `id` is the household id when Neon assigns one, otherwise the
  account id; `group` is `household`, `account`, or `other`; `name` is the first non-empty of
  household name, full name, or company name.
- **Recovering links the API omits.** Donations carry an account id but no household id, so
  household membership is recovered by joining the cleaned accounts on account id. Registrations
  likewise join accounts (for the household) and events (for the event's category), and fall back to
  a swept event id when the registration's own event id is missing.
- **Event categorization.** Each event is classified into a major category (performance / class /
  community / other) and, for performances, a minor category (theater / music / dance / opera /
  other) by ordered rules ported from the R project. An event that matches no rule is flagged
  `ERROR` on purpose, so anything uncategorized surfaces instead of hiding. See
  [Definitions](#definitions) for the categories themselves.
- **Manual overrides.** Curated one-off corrections live in `config/overrides.yaml`, kept separate
  from code.
- **Geographic cleaning.** Addresses are resolved to a point with a documented precision — a real
  street address first, then a household member's street address for PO-box accounts, then a ZIP
  centroid as a last resort, and `unmapped` if nothing works (see [Geography](#geography)). PO-box-only households are flagged so the tight distance counts stay honest.

## Definitions

How we select and define the people and events in this book. Every definition rolls a constituent up
to their household first (see [Data cleaning](#data-cleaning)); person-level counts are at the
household level.

- **Household.** The analytic unit: the household id when Neon assigns one, otherwise the single
  account id. A person in a Neon household is counted once with their housemates; a standalone
  account is its own household.
- **Donor.** A household whose total succeeded individual gifts are greater than zero. A gift counts
  when its status is `SUCCEEDED`, its type is `DONATION` or `PLEDBGEPAYMENT`, and the account is an
  `Individual`; we then drop gifts from accounts marked deceased or do-not-contact, and a known junk
  account (`36805`). Variables used: `donation_amount`, `donation_status`, `donation_type`,
  `account_type`, `deceased`, `do_not_contact`.
- **Patron.** A household with one or more performance registrations (`is_patron = n_performance >
  0`).
- **Class-taker.** A household with one or more class registrations (`is_class_taker = n_class > 0`).
- **Major category (performance / class / community / other).** Assigned from Neon's event category
  and the event name, in order, first match wins. For example, an event in a `Performances`,
  `Theater Performances`, `Music Performances`, or `Opera Performances` category is a performance
  (with a few named exceptions that are really classes); events in class or community categories fall
  to those buckets; anything unmatched is `other`. An event matching no rule at all is flagged
  `ERROR` as a tripwire.
- **Minor category (theater / music / dance / opera / other — performances only).** Assigned after
  the major category, again first match wins; anything unmatched is `other`. Theater and opera lean
  on Neon's category plus title patterns; music and dance use category plus title patterns.
  - *Theater* — category `Shakespeare` or `Theater Performances`, or a title matching a known theater
    production.
  - *Opera* — category `Opera Performances`, or the title contains "Opera".
  - *Music* — a Music category, or the title matches music patterns (Concert, Cabaret, named
    artists, and the like).
  - *Dance* — a Dance category or title.
- **Attendance (headcount).** A registration is a purchase, not a person; it averages about two
  attendees. Headcount counts ticket attendees whose registration status is `SUCCEEDED` (see
  [Attendance headcount](#attendance-headcount)).
- **Production and run.** A production is a title matched from the event name to a curated list; a
  run is that title's performances split when dates gap by more than 45 days (see
  [Reconstructing multi-day production runs](#reconstructing-multi-day-production-runs)).

## Donor size and persistence

- **Annual giving tiers.** A household's giving in a single year is placed in one of four bands:
  **<$100**, **$100–999**, **$1,000–4,999**, or **$5,000+**. These drive the over-time and
  persistence chapters. A household is counted once per year it gives (so a multi-year donor
  appears in several years).
- **Lifetime tiers.** Cumulative giving uses the same bands; households giving **$1,000+**
  lifetime are "large donors" and **$5,000+** are "major donors." These drive the preferences
  chapter, where lifetime giving measures the depth of the relationship.
- **Donor basis.** Size and persistence start from `succeeded_individual_gifts` (status
  `SUCCEEDED`, type `DONATION`/`PLEDBGEPAYMENT`, account type `Individual`) rolled up to the
  household — the same basis as `households_summary.total_donations`, so the figures reconcile
  with the headline donor counts.
- **Retention.** Of households that gave in year Y, the share that also gave in Y+1. The
  retention table drops the final year (it has no "next year"), and the trend drops the row whose
  next year is the partial current year. Retention is an **upper bound on voluntary churn**: a
  donor who has died also appears as "not retained."
- **Partial years.** The current calendar year is year-to-date and is left out of every
  over-time trend; where its figure is shown, it is labeled year-to-date and will rise as the
  year fills out.

## Geography

Distances are straight-line (haversine) from Hubbard Hall (25 E Main St, Cambridge NY) to each
household, bucketed into half-open mile bands. Households whose only address is a PO box are
placed at their ZIP's centroid and flagged "approximate"; they are excluded from the tight 1-mile
count so it stays honest.

## Attendance headcount

Registration counts understate attendance: one registration is a purchase, and averages about two
attendees. Where the text says **headcount**, it counts ticket attendees whose registration
status is SUCCEEDED — this also automatically drops canceled and refunded attendees.

## Reconstructing multi-day production runs

Each performance night is a separate Neon event (e.g. "Fun Home - Friday, February 3 at
7:30 pm") — there is no field linking the nights of a run. Runs are therefore reconstructed by a
manually curated mapping from event names to productions (`hh.analytics.productions`):

- **Mapping.** Every theater event name matches exactly one production title pattern (coverage is
  exhaustive and tested). For music and opera, only titles with multiple performances are mapped;
  one-off concerts cannot form a run and are dropped.
- **Runs.** A production's performances are split into separate runs when consecutive dates are
  more than 45 days apart — this separates recurring titles (Winter Carnival of New Work,
  Whispering Bones, Bread & Puppet) into their yearly runs.
- **Exclusions.** Events that were not regular public performances are dropped before mapping:
  canceled or moved dates, test events, open/dress/pay-what-you-will rehearsals, online
  productions, day-long special events, and weekday 10 am school shows.
- **Full-weekend runs.** The within-run day-of-week analysis keeps only runs that played all
  three of Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, so day comparisons hold the production constant.
- **Time slots.** A performance is classified a *matinee* if its name contains "2 pm" or
  "matinee"; otherwise *evening*.
